attributes.title: Shell Commands Refresher
attributes.description: Learn the essential terminal commands you'll use throughout this Git workshop.

post.html:
Why Learn the Shell?

Git is primarily a command-line tool.

Although graphical applications exist, understanding a few basic terminal commands will make working with Git much easier.

Throughout this workshop, you'll use the terminal to:

  • Navigate between folders
  • Create project files
  • Initialize Git repositories
  • Run Git commands

Don't worry—you only need a handful of commands to get started.






Important Note for Windows Users

Most examples in this workshop use a Unix-like shell.

If you are using Windows, you can follow along using:

  • Git Bash
  • Windows Terminal with WSL






Common Commands

Command Description
pwd Show the current directory
ls List files and folders
cd folder Enter a folder
cd .. Go to the parent folder
mkdir name Create a new directory
touch file Create an empty file
rm file Remove a file
clear Clear the terminal screen

📌 You can also press Ctrl + L in most terminals to clear the screen.






Moving Around Directories

Before using Git, it's important to know which directory you're currently in.

  • Show Current Directory

    pwd

  • List Files and Folders

    ls

  • Enter a Folder

    cd calculator-app

  • Go Back One Level

    cd ..

This moves you to the parent directory.






Creating Files and Folders

  • Create a new folder:

    mkdir my-project

  • Create a new file:

    touch main.py

  • Remove a file:

    rm main.py






Be Careful with rm

The following command deletes a file:

rm file.txt

Unlike dragging a file to the Recycle Bin, terminal deletion is often permanent.

Always double-check before using rm.






A Small Practice Exercise

Open your terminal and run the following commands:

mkdir git-practice
cd git-practice

touch app.py
touch README.md

ls

echo "Hello, World!" >> README.md
cat README.md

pwd

Now you know the basics of the shell! Let's move on to Git.
post.markdown:
## Why Learn the Shell? Git is primarily a **command-line tool**. Although graphical applications exist, understanding a few basic terminal commands will make working with Git much easier. Throughout this workshop, you'll use the terminal to: - Navigate between folders - Create project files - Initialize Git repositories - Run Git commands Don't worry—you only need a handful of commands to get started.




## Important Note for Windows Users Most examples in this workshop use a Unix-like shell. If you are using Windows, you can follow along using: - Git Bash - Windows Terminal with WSL




## Common Commands | Command | Description | | ------------ | -------------------------- | | `pwd` | Show the current directory | | `ls` | List files and folders | | `cd folder` | Enter a folder | | `cd ..` | Go to the parent folder | | `mkdir name` | Create a new directory | | `touch file` | Create an empty file | | `rm file` | Remove a file | | `clear` | Clear the terminal screen | 📌 You can also press `Ctrl + L` in most terminals to clear the screen.




## Moving Around Directories Before using Git, it's important to know which directory you're currently in. - Show Current Directory ```bash pwd ``` - List Files and Folders ```bash ls ``` - Enter a Folder ```bash cd calculator-app ``` - Go Back One Level ```bash cd .. ``` This moves you to the parent directory.




## Creating Files and Folders - Create a new folder: ```bash mkdir my-project ``` - Create a new file: ```bash touch main.py ``` - Remove a file: ```bash rm main.py ```




## Be Careful with `rm` The following command deletes a file: ```bash rm file.txt ``` Unlike dragging a file to the Recycle Bin, terminal deletion is often permanent. Always double-check before using `rm`.




## A Small Practice Exercise Open your terminal and run the following commands: ```bash mkdir git-practice cd git-practice touch app.py touch README.md ls echo "Hello, World!" >> README.md cat README.md pwd ``` Now you know the basics of the shell! Let's move on to Git.
post.title: Shell Commands Refresher
